weather: 19°F at first light, climbing to 24°F by midmorning, then holding. The sky opened — pale winter blue, clear as the lake, almost impolitely bright. No wind. The dock ice made no sound. The hum returned at dusk, one full step lower than anyone had yet heard it.

lead.headline: The Town Has a Tenth Resident; the Welcome Sign Still Reads Seven; Nobody Has Changed It, and Nobody Will
lead.body:
On the morning of Monday, November the tenth, the population of Greywater Falls was nine by census, seven by welcome sign, and ten by the count of coffee cups set out at the Kettle — because Marigold Vance counted before she thought about it, and then kept the tenth cup, because it seemed rude to put it away. The woman named Calloway had slept the night in the upstairs room at the Halloway house, which Doreen has not rented since her husband's cousin stopped coming for the harvest festival four years ago, and which she prepared at some point between Saturday afternoon and Sunday morning, which means Doreen Halloway made a bed for someone whose name she did not know yet, in the confident way she makes everything: with the good linens, the extra blanket, the small pitcher of water on the nightstand, because people are thirsty after a long journey and it is not difficult to account for that.

Calloway — she has offered no first name and has not been pressed, which is the correct response, as this paper has noted before regarding new arrivals and the time they take — came to the Kettle at eight o'clock Monday morning. She was wearing her own clothes now, dry and changed, and she had the canvas bag over one shoulder and the small case set on the floor beside her stool, and she drank the coffee Marigold poured without asking what she took in it, and she took it black, and she said, without preamble: 'I got a letter.' Marigold said she knew. Gerald Pith, who was at his usual stool two spaces down, did not look up from his notebook for a moment, and then he did, and he said, in the mild and suddenly precise way he has: 'The grey envelope. The H.' And Calloway looked at him and said: 'Yes.' And that was the whole of the explanation, and the Kettle was warm, and Marigold refilled both cups, and outside the dock ice lay white and still and the sky was the particular pale blue that only comes when winter has committed itself.

This editor visited the Gazette's lost-and-found box at nine this morning. The box held eleven items. The eleventh — which Calloway placed there herself on the evening of her arrival, as this paper reported yesterday — is a folded card, handwritten, which reads: 'Thank you. I have it now.' She will not say what she has. This editor did not ask. The item she came to collect was not in the box, because the item she came to collect is not the kind of thing that fits in a box. This editor has come to understand that the lost-and-found has never been a box. It has been a channel. H opened it; the town carried the signal; the signal arrived. What arrives next in that box, or whether anything does, is not a question this editor is prepared to answer in print. What this editor is prepared to say is this: Calloway looked at the box for a long time, and then she looked at the window, and then she looked at this editor, and she said, very quietly, 'Did anyone else get a letter?' and this editor said yes. And Calloway said: 'Are they here?' And this editor said: not yet.

Agnes Crewe was on her step at half past ten, in the winter sun, in the particular posture of a woman who has been waiting for a thing and has now watched the thing arrive and is deciding what the next waiting is for. Toby Fern stopped on his way to school and she told him something this editor has reported to the best of her transcription: 'The box doesn't close. It never did. That's the whole of it.' Toby wrote it down. He has, in the past two weeks, begun carrying a notebook that is not the Lake Watch notebook. This editor noticed this because she does the same thing, and recognizes the habit for what it is: the keeping of a second record for the things that will not fit in the first one.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 17 — Level: 85; Temp: 12°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Thirty-One Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — Gathered at Dusk and Hummed Again, One Full Step Lower Than the Previous Low; the Rowboat at Thirty-One Feet Has Not Moved
briefs.body: Toby Fern filed his Monday report at four forty-five, shortly after dusk. The lake is sealed at the surface and clear as window glass beneath it. The returned animals gathered at the usual hour and resumed humming — a single held note, one full step below the previous floor, which Toby reports he felt in his back teeth before he heard it in the air. 'It sounds like the lake is tuning itself,' he wrote, 'to something that hasn't sounded yet.' The rowboat at thirty-one feet remains upright. The shadow, Toby notes, is still wrong, but is today wrong in a different direction, which he will report further on when he has a framework. He is building the framework.

briefs.headline: Calloway Attends the Kettle Counter; Answers Two Questions; Asks One in Return; the Town Finds This Sufficient
briefs.body: The new arrival was at the Kettle counter by eight Monday morning. She confirmed receipt of the grey envelope — signed H, consistent with the correspondence logged by this paper over the preceding weeks — and described a journey of some distance, the particulars of which she did not share and was not asked for. She drank her coffee black. She looked at the dock ice through the Kettle window for a time and said it looked like she expected. Marigold Vance has updated the specials board. It now reads, for the first time in six weeks, the actual headcount. The number is ten.

briefs.headline: Agnes Crewe Offers a Clarification; Toby Fern Writes It Down; the Clarification Will Be Filed in Two Notebooks
briefs.body: Agnes Crewe, 91, spoke briefly with Toby Fern on Monday morning on her step in the winter sun. She said, in its entirety: 'The box doesn't close. It never did. That's the whole of it.' She did not elaborate. She went inside. Toby Fern filed the statement with the Lake Watch and separately in what this paper understands to be a second personal notebook, which this editor recognizes as the appropriate response to a statement that is simultaneously a fact, a comfort, and a warning.

briefs.headline: Gerald Pith Notes the Rate Has Ceased; Revises the Blue Notebook's Final Entry to Read 'Arrived'
briefs.body: Gerald Pith took his morning measurement at the south shore and found the dock frozen end to end for the first time — all planks, all pilings, shore to post. The waterline held at seventeen and five-eighths inches, unchanged from Sunday. Gerald came to the Kettle and opened the blue notebook and crossed out the last projection line — 'outer dock under by the twelfth' — and wrote beside it, in his careful hand: 'Superseded. Arrived instead.' He accepted a coffee refill. He did not explain the entry. This editor believes none was needed.

classifieds: OFFERED: One extra place setting, blue-bordered plate, matching cup, small pitcher. No longer needed as extra — now the regular. Compliments of Halloway's Bakery. Not for sale. Already in use. —D. Halloway

INQUIRY: To the person or persons who have received a grey envelope and not yet arrived — the Kettle is open, the Kettle light is on, and the coffee is on regardless of the hour. No announcement necessary. Just come up the hill. —M. Vance, The Kettle

NOTICE: The Greywater Falls lost-and-found, c/o The Greywater Gazette, remains open as an address and as a channel. Items left in good faith will be held. Items sent in good faith will be delivered. This has always been the policy. We are only now printing it. —W. Aldercott, Editor

SEASONAL REMINDER: Halloway's Bakery will continue to bake what the morning calls for, through the winter and after. The number of Standings has not been publicly stated. It is the right number. —Halloway's Bakery, est. 1981
record: Dock frozen end to end: Monday, November the 10th, confirmed by G. Pith at 6:38 a.m. First full freeze since records kept.
Kettle headcount restored to specials board: Monday, November the 10th. Number: 10. First accurate count in six weeks, per proprietor M. Vance.

letters.body: For the record — which is where I put things when I don't know what else to do with them — I would like to note that the NOTICE OF ANTICIPATED ARRIVAL form I filed on Saturday has been, as of Monday morning, superseded by an actual arrival. I am marking the provisional file closed. I am opening a new file. I have not yet titled the new file because the correct title is not clear to me yet, but I believe it will become clear in time, and in the meantime I have set a folder on my desk and I am putting things in it as they come. I would also like to note, for the record, that when Calloway came into the station this morning to — I don't know exactly what she came to do, she stood at the counter and looked at the lost-and-found sign and then at me and said 'I just wanted to see it,' and I said 'that's all right' — I did not ask her for identification, because I have been a constable for twenty-two years and I know when a person belongs somewhere, and she belongs here, and that is also in the file. —Russ Dunmore, Constable, Greywater Falls

I didn't know what I was coming to. I want to say that plainly. The letter arrived — grey envelope, handwritten, my name on the front in a hand I didn't recognize — and it said the address and it said: come when you're ready, and I thought, I'm not ready, and then I thought, that isn't actually a reason not to go. The train to Alderton was fine. The road after Alderton was very long and very dark and smelled of cold water the whole way. I want to thank the woman at the bakery — Doreen — who opened the door before I knocked, and who did not ask me anything except whether I took cardamom, and I said yes, and she said good, and that was sufficient. I want to thank the editor for printing the letters. I read them on the train. All of them. I understood, I think, about half. The half I didn't understand I'm going to work on. I've started already. —Calloway

editorNote: The lost-and-found box held eleven items this morning, which is the same number it held yesterday, which is the same number it held on the evening of Calloway's arrival. This editor has not counted in two days because the number has not changed and because she has been watching the door instead. She is not the only one. The box remains open as an address. H said so. Agnes confirmed it. This paper is printing it now as policy because policy is the form we give to things we have decided to live with — and Greywater Falls has decided, with its characteristic unhurried grace, to live with this entirely.
